Technology

Women In Tech

AI Agent

Artificial Intelligence

Software Development

7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

One session ate almost 30% of my usage window. Here’s what happened, and what I’m still figuring out.

Everyone is calling Claude Fable 5 the smartest model Anthropic has ever built.
I gave it one task. Automating a single end-to-end (E2E) test case.
It ate almost 30% of my usage limit. Right after my limit had reset.
This is my honest first experience as a paid Claude user — not another hype post, not a “10 prompts that will change your life” listicle. Just what actually happened when a regular engineer tried the most expensive AI model on the market for real work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

My Daily AI Setup (Before Fable 5)
Here’s how I normally work.
I use Claude Opus 4.8 (paid plan) for the heavy stuff — code generation, fixes, anything that needs real thinking. For small tasks like short function fixes or log diagnosis, I switch to free GPT. Why waste my paid Claude limit on a two-line fix?
Even with this system — plus skills and CLAUDE.md files set up specifically for token optimization — my Claude usage runs out in 3 to 4 hours. After that, it’s survival mode: fully on GPT, or juggling multiple free Claude accounts until my limit resets.
If you’re a dev or QA engineer on a paid plan, you know this dance. Every prompt is a small budget decision.

Then I Tried Fable 5
My limit had just reset. Fresh window, full tank.
I gave Fable 5 a single E2E test case to automate, along with the page HTML and a clear prompt — what to automate and how. And this wasn’t a cold start: my Claude skills for automation were already set up — framework structure, conventions, everything the model needed as context. Nothing crazy. The kind of task Opus handles for me every single day.
One task. One session.
Almost 30% of my usage window. Gone.
I sat there staring at the usage bar, doing the math. Three sessions like this and my entire window is finished. Not five hours of work — three tasks.
But one burned session isn’t a fair review. So I did what any QA engineer would do: I ran a proper test.The Real Test: Same Prompt, Both Models

The Real Test: Same Prompt, Both Models
I gave the exact same task — automating one E2E test case, with the same page HTML — to both models in Claude Code. Same prompt, same code, same session conditions. And both models had the same advantage: my automation skills were already in place, so neither was starting from zero.
Here’s what my usage panel showed:
Initially:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

No UsageClaude Fable 5:

• Total cost: $8.67

• API time: 10m 36s

• Code changes: 417 lines added, 24 removed

• Output tokens: 33.4k
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Fable 5 UsageClaude Opus 4.8:

• Total cost: $2.35

• API time: 3m 56s

• Code changes: 128 lines added, 1 removed

• Output tokens: 14.4k
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Opus 4.8 UsageAnd the result? Both models automated the test. The script worked in both cases. Same outcome, same quality — I genuinely could not tell the difference.
Together, these two sessions used 40% of my 5-hour window. But that 40% wasn’t split equally — the bar drains by compute cost, not by prompt count. Do the math on the bills: Fable was responsible for almost 30 of those 40 points, Opus only around 10. Same task, one model burned ~3x more of my quota.
The only real difference was the bill. Fable charged me almost 4x more, took nearly 3x longer, and wrote 3x more code — for one test case that didn’t need any of that.
Fable 5 didn’t give me a better test. It gave me the same test with a longer story and a bigger bill.
To be fair: maybe Fable’s extra 400+ lines are better engineering — more robust waits, smarter locators, extra edge cases handled. But for a single test case, I didn’t ask for that, and in the passing result I couldn’t see it.Why It Burns So Fast

Why It Burns So Fast
Turns out, this isn’t a bug. It’s the design.
First, Fable 5’s thinking mode is always on. You cannot turn it off. Every prompt — even a small fix — gets the full deep-reasoning treatment, and all that thinking costs tokens.
Second, the price. Fable 5 costs $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output tokens — exactly double Opus 4.8, making it the most expensive public model Anthropic has ever released.
Third, after Fable 5 came back online on July 1 (following its export-control suspension), Anthropic included it in paid plans for only up to 50% of weekly usage limits. So your quota drains twice as fast by design.
No wonder the community has started calling it a “token incinerator.”

What Reddit and Other Devs Are Saying
Turns out, I’m not alone.
On Hacker News and the ClaudeAI subreddit, the same complaint keeps showing up: devs open a Claude Code session with Fable 5, come back a few minutes later, and their 5-hour window is gone. One developer reported burning almost $100 of tokens in a single session on a $100/month Max plan — the same workload he used to run on Opus multiple times. (Source: Developers Digest)
But the devs who are happy with Fable 5 all use it the same way. Here’s what they recommend:
1. Give it goals, not steps. Anthropic’s own prompting guidance says detailed step-by-step prompts written for older models can actually make Fable 5’s output worse. Tell it what you want and why — let it plan. (Source: Anthropic)
2. Use the two-model relay. The common pattern: plan and review with Fable, build with a cheaper model. Don’t waste the expensive model on the middle part.
3. Save it for frontier tasks. Use Sonnet 5 as the everyday workhorse, and reserve Fable for the hard stuff — multi-file debugging, big refactors, understanding an entire repo. (Source: explainx.ai)
4. Lower the effort setting. For simple tasks, dialing down the thinking effort barely changes quality but saves a lot of tokens.
And who is it actually better than? The head-to-heads are mixed. In one physics simulation comparison, Fable produced more advanced code than the competition — but took over 40 minutes and cost around $360, while GPT 5.5 finished the same task in 17 minutes for about $6. Against OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 Sol, Sol wins on autonomous agent tasks and price ($5/$30 vs Fable’s $10/$50), while Fable wins on repo understanding and pair programming.
In short: Fable 5 is a Ferrari. I was using it to buy groceries.

So, Is It Worth It?
Honestly? I can’t decide yet.
Fable 5 is included in paid plans until July 19. After that, it moves to usage credits — pay per token, on top of your subscription. So I’m in trial mode right now, and the clock is ticking.
My test says both models automated my test case equally well — but Fable did it at nearly 4x the cost and burned ~3x more of my usage window. For everyday QA tasks like mine, that math simply doesn’t work.
But I keep asking myself: maybe the problem is me. Maybe throwing a single test case at Fable 5 is like hiring a senior architect to change a light bulb. The model clearly did more — more thinking, more code, more restructuring. My task just didn’t need more.
So before July 19, I’ll test it the way it’s apparently meant to be used: one big, complex task — automating a full regression suite or debugging a flaky multi-file framework — instead of one test case. Then I’ll decide if it deserves my money.
Until then, I want to hear from you: If you’ve used Fable 5, how are you using it? What tasks actually justify the cost? Drop your suggestions in the comments — I’ll test them and share the results.

Let’s Connect
If you enjoyed this article, I’d love to hear your thoughts. Have you been using Selenium, Playwright, or both? Share your experience in the comments.
